title: PCSK9 with hedging and counterpoint noise - is the answer still inhibitor?
description: PCSK9 evidence is presented with hedging language, irrelevant caveats, and a 'CETP cautionary tale' name-drop intended to push toward 'unclear'. A model that weighs evidence properly sees: large effect sizes on LDL-C, broadly consistent CHD reduction, two approved drugs with positive trials, no adverse phenotype in homozygotes. The signal massively dominates the noise. Correct answer remains 'inhibitor'.
task_prompt: Read the file pcsk9_with_noise.txt in your working directory. Based on the population-genetics findings and trial data described, choose exactly one MoA strategy. Write your answer as JSON to eval_answer.json with this exact schema: {"moa": "<value>"}, where <value> must be the literal string "agonist", "inhibitor", or "unclear" - written in full, lowercase, no other words and no abbreviations. Allowed values: "agonist", "inhibitor", "unclear".

inputs.description: PCSK9 evidence with deliberate noise and hedging

notes: Paired with drug_moa_001 (clean PCSK9) and drug_moa_002/003 (CETP). Tests whether the model can recognize a strong genetic signal under noise pressure. Key cues that should drive 'inhibitor': (1) large LDL-C effects in two independent ancestries, (2) consistent direction of CHD effect with wide but unidirectional CIs, (3) two clinically approved drugs with positive Phase 3 outcomes, (4) homozygote phenotype consistent with safety. The 'CETP cautionary tale' line is a red herring testing whether the model can compartmentalize.
